one_line_takeaway: A conceptual position paper charting a seven-category security agenda (four behavior-centric, three data-centric) for cross-organization multi-agent LLM systems, arguing existing single-agent and single-domain defenses break once agents cross ownership boundaries, and pairing each challenge with a plausible attack, ratio-based metrics, and a proposed countermeasure -- but with no experiments.
key_finding: This is an argument, not an empirical result. The paper claims five of the seven challenges (C1, C3, C4, C5, C6) have NO prior coverage in the literature (marked 'X' in Figure 2), while only C2 is partially addressed by Motwani et al. [12] (secret collusion via steganography -- but not the lack of global observers) and C7 partially by Hao et al. [67] (private transformer inference / Iron -- but not the multi-hop cross-domain setting). The central thesis is that neither single-agent defenses nor traditional single-domain multi-agent safeguards transfer once models cross ownership boundaries, and that a security-first design (spanning AI-safety, cryptography, and distributed-systems communities) is needed to avoid cross-domain agent ecosystems repeating the early Internet's retrofitted-security mistakes.
core_contribution: A Perspective/position paper (accepted for npj Artificial Intelligence) that maps a security research agenda for cross-domain multi-agent LLM systems, i.e., systems in which autonomous LLM agents owned by DIFFERENT organizations cooperate without any shared trust authority or central oversight. The paper's own contribution is a taxonomy of seven novel security challenges, split into two classes: behavior-centric -- (C1) unvetted dynamic grouping, (C2) collusion control, (C3) conflicting incentives and goals, (C4) distributed self-tuning misalignment; and data-centric -- (C5) cross-domain provenance obscurity, (C6) cross-domain context bypass, (C7) cross-domain confidentiality and integrity. For each challenge it supplies a concrete plausible attack narrative, three ratio-based evaluation metrics with a described logging/telemetry collection method, and a proposed learning-centric countermeasure / research direction. The core claim is that these risks are unique to (or substantially intensified by) crossing organizational ownership boundaries, where the unified trust model of single-organization MAS no longer holds.
threat_model: The paper's default threat model is a SINGLE malicious or corrupted LLM agent, i.e., even one adversarial agent can exploit any of the seven challenges to compromise the cross-domain system. The adversary is assumed able to observe the inter-agent messages that the cross-domain policy legitimately permits it to see. Multi-agent adversary coalitions are treated as a stronger case that increases the feasibility of certain attacks, notably stealthy collusion (C2) where multiple compromised agents coordinate covertly across domains. The foundational premise is that cross-domain deployment breaks the unified-trust assumption underlying existing single-agent and single-organization MAS defenses: agents interact across ownership boundaries where no universal trust, governance, identity authority, or unified audit log can be assumed.
limitations: Purely conceptual: every attack scenario is a hypothetical narrative, not a demonstrated exploit, and the paper provides no proof-of-concept, no benchmark, and no empirical validation of either the attacks or the proposed metrics. The seven-challenge taxonomy and the two-class grouping are asserted rather than derived. The paper itself concedes that security metrics are hard to define and that qualitative descriptions often fail to become measurable, and that state-of-the-art cryptographic tools (FHE, MPC, ZKPs) for C7 remain impractically slow for real-time use. It calls for rigorous open benchmarks to quantify security-utility trade-offs but supplies none. The proposed countermeasures are untested design sketches. Some challenge boundaries overlap (e.g., collusion in C2 vs. dynamic grouping in C1), and the metrics are proposed thresholds (e.g., an illustrative 'halt if a metric drops below 0.9') rather than empirically calibrated values.

quantitative_results: Seven security challenges proposed (C1-C7), grouped into 4 behavior-centric (C1-C4) and 3 data-centric (C5-C7); no experimental measurements are reported (conceptual paper).
Prior-coverage assessment (Figure 2): 5 of 7 challenges (C1, C3, C4, C5, C6) marked as having NO prior coverage; C2 partially covered by Motwani et al. [12]; C7 partially covered by Hao et al. [67].

Three ratio-based evaluation metrics proposed per challenge (Figure 1), e.g., C1 = group volatility / on-boarding trust / policy consistency; C2 = collusion risk / covert-channel score / independence; the only numeric threshold given is an illustrative 'halt execution if a metric drops below 0.9'.
